summary:
The Founding of Batavia (1619 AD) established the headquarters of the [[event:2357|Dutch East India Company]] (VOC) on the northwestern coast of Java — the city that would become modern Jakarta and serve as the colonial capital of the Dutch East Indies until Indonesian independence in 1945. Jan Pieterszoon Coen, the VOC's most brutal and effective Governor-General, seized the site of the Jayakarta trading post, destroyed the existing settlement, and built a fortified Dutch city modeled on Amsterdam, complete with canals, warehouses, and a central castle. Coen's methods were extraordinarily violent — his massacre of the Banda Islands population (1621) to impose a nutmeg monopoly killed an estimated 90% of the 15,000 inhabitants.

Batavia served as the nerve center of the VOC's vast Asian trading network, connecting the spice islands of Maluku, the pepper ports of Sumatra, the textile markets of India, the silver trade of Japan, and the Chinese trade at Canton into a single commercial system managed from Java. The city attracted Chinese merchants, craftsmen, and laborers who became an essential workforce; the Chinese community's periodic tensions with Dutch authorities culminated in the Batavia Massacre of 1740, in which Dutch soldiers and Indonesian mobs killed an estimated 5,000 Chinese residents — an atrocity that briefly shook confidence in Dutch colonial governance. Despite such brutalities, Batavia/Jakarta grew from a trading fort into a major city and remained the administrative, commercial, and cultural center of the Indonesian archipelago, a legacy of the VOC's 18th-century dominance that persisted long after Dutch colonial rule ended.
